title: Yarrow
subtitle: The wound herb of old country medicine
excerpt: Achillea millefolium: styptic, sweat-promoting, and inflammation-calming. One of the oldest recorded wound herbs in the western tradition, and a reliable fever herb in acute colds. Requires careful identification when foraging.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, yarrow entry: the styptic and wound tradition, Achilles myth, the fever and sweat-promoting use. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, yarrow entry, the wound and ague traditions. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Achillea millefolium, the haemostatic and sweat-promoting uses.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that slows or stops bleeding when applied externally. Yarrow is the classic styptic of western herbalism, traditionally pressed onto minor cuts and grazes.
A herb that promotes sweating. Traditionally used at the onset of a fever or cold to support the body's own response. Taken as a hot infusion and combined with warmth.

content.text: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) grows in meadows, roadsides, and rough grass across temperate Europe, Asia, and North America. Its leaves are feathery and deeply divided. Upright stems carry flat-topped clusters of small white or pink flowers from June to November. The plant smells strongly when you bruise a leaf. Its Latin name refers to Achilles, who in myth used it to stop his soldiers' wounds bleeding. The wound herb use is real and ancient: yarrow has been found at Neanderthal burial sites.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy. Yarrow is in the daisy family. Readers with a known ragweed, chrysanthemum, or daisy allergy should avoid, or patch-test before external use.

Pregnancy. Yarrow has documented uterine-stimulating (uterine-stimulating) activity at therapeutic doses and is avoided in pregnancy. Consult a herbalist or your midwife before any therapeutic use of yarrow in pregnancy.

Photosensitivity (rare). The aromatic oil in fresh yarrow can rarely cause a photosensitising skin reaction in sensitive individuals who handle the fresh plant and then expose the skin to sun. Dried herb at standard infusion doses does not carry this risk.

Foraging caution. Wild yarrow can be confused with poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) and wild carrot (Daucus carota). Yarrow has feathery aromatic leaves; hemlock has a distinctive mousy smell and purple-blotched stems. If you cannot positively identify the plant, use cultivated dried yarrow from a reputable supplier.

content.text: Latin binomial: Achillea millefolium. Family: Asteraceae. Parts used: aerial parts (leaf, stem, flower), harvested when in flower (June to October). Dried for internal use; fresh for immediate compress or poultice. Height 20-60 cm. The feathery leaves are diagnostic, as is the aromatic smell. Hemlock (Conium maculatum) is the dangerous look-alike: it lacks the aromatic smell entirely and has smooth, not feathery, leaves.
The key active compounds include the aromatic oil (azulene, cineole, camphor), the sesquiterpene compounds, flavonoids, achilleine (an alkaloid associated with the

action (promoting sweating in fever) comes from the aromatic oil and flavonoids. The inflammation-calming action is associated with the sesquiterpene compounds and flavonoids. Tannins support both the wound-staunching use and the use for heavy menstrual bleeding.

Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer) documents yarrow as a wound herb for staunching bleeding in cuts, as a sweat-promoting herb for fevers, and as an astringent for heavy menstrual bleeding. She records it as one of the most widely-used herbs in English country medicine. Culpeper, the 17th-century herbalist, calls it a reliable wound herb and records a use for recurrent fever.

Native American peoples used yarrow for wounds, fever, and colds, the same uses the European tradition developed. The Eclectic American physicians also document the wound-staunching and sweat-promoting uses.

For wounds and minor bleeding: fresh yarrow crushed and pressed onto the cut is the oldest form. A compress of strong infusion (10 g dried herb in 200 ml water, steeped covered 10 minutes, cooled slightly) applied to the wound is the alternative when fresh herb is unavailable. For fever and colds: a hot infusion of 2-4 g dried herb in 200 ml water, steeped covered 10 minutes, taken hot with blanket rest to encourage sweating. For menstrual support: the same infusion, drunk at room temperature.
Pregnancy: avoid therapeutic doses. Daisy allergy: avoid. Fresh herb rarely causes sun-sensitivity. Any heavy bleeding, persistent fever, or significant wound needs medical assessment.
